does: This paper proposes a novel calibration method for Process Reward Models (PRMs) used in inference-time scaling by applying conditional optimal transport (CondOT) to learn monotonic conditional quantile functions. The approach conditions on PRM hidden states to estimate calibrated success probability quantiles, enabling extraction of confidence bounds at arbitrary levels. The method is integrated into the instance-adaptive scaling framework and evaluated on mathematical reasoning tasks including MATH-500 and AIME benchmarks, demonstrating improved calibration compared to uncalibrated PRMs and standard quantile regression baselines, with corresponding improvements in downstream Best-of-N performance.
connects: This work addresses a critical limitation of existing Process Reward Models, which are known to be poorly calibrated and overestimate success probabilities—a key bottleneck in inference-time scaling methods that sample and select among multiple candidate outputs. The paper builds on recent advances in conditional optimal transport for supervised learning and the instance-adaptive scaling framework that leverages confidence estimates to allocate computational budgets. It extends quantile regression approaches for uncertainty quantification by imposing structural monotonicity constraints through optimal transport, connecting to broader lines of work on calibration in machine learning and confidence estimation for decision-making under uncertainty.
enables: By establishing conditional optimal transport as an effective calibration tool for reward models, this work opens possibilities for more reliable uncertainty quantification in AI reasoning systems and other domains using learned reward signals. The method's ability to extract calibrated confidence bounds at arbitrary levels could improve inference-time scaling strategies beyond mathematical reasoning to other complex reasoning tasks. Future directions include extending the approach to other model architectures, investigating whether the method scales to harder problems and domains, and exploring how better-calibrated confidence estimates might enable more sophisticated adaptive computation strategies that optimally trade off quality versus computational cost in real-world applications.
